channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: China Seeks A.I. Independence, Weakening Trump’s Leverage
item.description: Before this week’s U.S.-Chinese summit, Beijing reached a milestone in its quest for technological self-sufficiency.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: DeepSeek said for the first time that its new model had been optimized to run on chips made by the Chinese tech giant Huawei.

item.title: China Increasingly Views Trump’s America as an Empire in Decline
item.description: For decades, many Chinese viewed the United States with a mix of admiration, envy and resentment. President Trump’s volatile second term shattered that image.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:description: The port at Tianjin, China. In response to President Trump’s tariffs, China has become more assertive on the global stage.

item.title: Byron Allen Is Buying a Controlling Stake in BuzzFeed
item.description: Jonah Peretti, BuzzFeed’s co-founder 20 years ago, will step down as chief executive and serve as its president of A.I.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Bethany Mollenkof for The New York Times
item.media:description: A company associated with Byron Allen, shown in 2019, will pay $120 million for a 52 percent stake in BuzzFeed.

item.title: Nations Brace for Long-Term Economic Woes as Trump Calls Iran Truce Plan ‘Garbage’
item.description: President Trump said the fragile cease-fire was on “life support,” and India’s leader urged residents to cut back on purchases and travel.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Sanam Mahoozi, Michael Levenson and Max Bearak
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran last week. Iranian officials are facing domestic pressure to reach a deal as the country’s economic problems have grown only more dire since the war.

item.title: Dean Buntrock, Maestro of Waste Management, Dies at 94
item.description: Anticipating how the environmental movement would affect trash disposal in the United States, he built a 12-truck garbage company into a coast-to-coast behemoth.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:description: Dean Buntrock in 1984. He foresaw that an increase in environmental regulation would require a more capital-intensive garbage industry.

item.title: Trump Proposes Suspending Federal Gas Tax Until Prices Fall
item.description: Congress would have to agree to any suspension, and dropping federal taxes would not make up for the steep increase in prices since the war began.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gas prices have risen about 50 percent since the United States and Israel began the war with Iran on Feb. 28.

item.title: RFK Jr. Is Driving a Vast Inquiry Into Vaccines, Despite His Public Silence
item.description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has toned down his public criticism of vaccines, under orders from the White House. But inside his department, a sprawling research effort is a top priority.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: First-Time Home Buyers Are Staying on the Sidelines as Costs Rise
item.description: The effects of the war in Iran have raised mortgage rates and lowered consumer confidence, making it even harder for house hunters.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sebastian Siadecki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lillian Rouse and her fiancé, Mark Debney, in their rental home in North Carolina. Ms. Rouse said that delaying a home purchase had also meant putting off starting a family.

item.title: How the U.S. Is Trying to Ensure the Dollar’s Dominance During Economic Turmoil
item.description: As the government has been devising plans to keep the dollar dominant, China has been making its own moves to increase global influence of the renminbi.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has been leading the talks over the currency swaps.

item.title: Britain Will Nationalize Its Last Major Steel Mill, Prime Minister Says
item.description: Keir Starmer, with thousands of jobs at stake, vowed to take full control of British Steel, a year after the government first intervened to keep its operations going.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:description: Last year, the prime minister, Keir Starmer, visited the British Steel facility in Scunthorpe. The plant is responsible for thousands of jobs.

item.title: Private Equity Lashes Out at New Research on Carried Interest
item.description: Estimates by researchers at Yale found that closing the carried interest loophole could lead to billions more in tax revenues than previously estimated.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Grady McGregor
item.media:credit: Stefani Reynolds for The New York Times
item.media:description: The debate over closing the tax loophole on carried interest just took a new twist.

item.title: Dua Lipa Sues Samsung Over Use of Her Image on TV Packaging
item.description: The pop star is suing the electronics giant for more than $15 million, accusing it of using her image without authorization.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Jean Baptiste Lacroix/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Dua Lipa at an event in Los Angeles in March.

item.title: NBC to Turn Wordle Into a TV Game Show, With Savannah Guthrie as Host
item.description: The word puzzle published by The New York Times is the inspiration for a game show that will begin airing next year. Jimmy Fallon is a producer.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum

item.title: Will a Ban on Bromated Flour Change New York Bagels and Pizza?
item.description: Bromated flour, which makes breads and crusts stretchy and springy (and cheap), is among the carcinogens forbidden in pending state legislation.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin
item.media:credit: Mimi d’Autremont for The New York Times
item.media:description: At Utopia Bagels, in Whitestone, Queens, they’ll have to change the recipe if New York’s governor signs legislation banning potassium bromate.

item.title: As Trump Heads to Beijing, China Is ‘Locked and Loaded’ for a Fight
item.description: Beijing is signaling that it is ready for a trade showdown, and it is building up a legal arsenal in preparation.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.media:description: The port in Ningbo, China. “We are on the brink of a much more frequent or widespread use of Chinese countermeasures against U.S. sanctions,” said Andrew Gillholm, a China expert at Control Risks.

item.title: Spirit Was the Only Airline in Town. Now What?
item.description: Spirit’s presence was a point of pride in Latrobe, Pa., where the security lines were short and airport parking is free. Residents are mourning the loss.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:description: The terminal at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport, near Latrobe, Pa., has sat mostly empty since Spirit Airlines shut down this month.

item.title: ‘It’s Getting Unfriendlier’: International Students Race to Find Jobs
item.description: Students say they have been passed over for jobs and interviews because of visa restrictions. Some have a Plan B: leaving the United States.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin and Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s just getting unfriendlier and unfriendlier,” said Caroline Liu, a Chinese citizen and student at M.I.T.

item.title: Why Two Big Companies Just Cut Paid Family Leave
item.description: Deloitte and Zoom are among the employers reducing support for working parents, signaling a retreat from family-friendly benefits.
item.dc:creator: Claire Cain Miller
item.media:credit: Jonah Markowitz for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. is the only industrialized nation not to have paid family leave as a federal benefit.

item.title: Walmart, Target and Dollar General Spend Billions to Make Stores Look Nicer
item.description: Walmart, Target and Dollar General are among those investing in remodeling thousands of existing stores, even as shoppers take their dollars online.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: A renovated Target store in Paramus, N.J. The improvements are part of a $5 billion capital investment plan at the chain.

item.title: Oil Prices Rise as Prospects for U.S.-Iran Peace Deal Fizzle
item.description: Oil prices rose and stock futures ticked down as investors reacted after the two sides failed to agree on a U.S.-Iran peace deal.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump Administration Is Considering Pausing the Federal Gas Tax to Lower Prices, Energy Secretary Says
item.description: The tax is a little over 18 cents a gallon for gasoline. With the average national price above $4.50, the measure will provide little relief.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: The average national price for a gallon of gasoline was $4.52 on Sunday, according to the AAA motor club.

item.title: How Brandeis Is Trying to Change College Shopping
item.description: A new tool on the university’s website tells you what the first year “will” cost if you get in.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber

item.title: Consumers Lean on a ‘Hamster Wheel’ of Credit to Manage Rising Costs
item.description: As prices increase for gas, groceries and other staples, more and more households are borrowing to get by.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: The cost of a gallon of gas near Mr. Watts’s home rose 70 cents overnight one day late last month.

item.title: Why Some People Are Allergic to ‘Peanut Butter Raises’
item.description: The beloved spread has become a metaphor for things that are less than delicious.
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley

item.title: Sales Are Up. Celebrities Are In. Is Gap Officially Back?
item.description: Richard Dickson has drawn inspiration from the clothing retailer’s early days as he tries to regain its cultural cachet.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times
item.media:description: Richard Dickson joined Gap from Mattel, where he revitalized brands like Barbie, which he helped get to the big screen.

item.title: All Those A.I. Note Takers? They’re Making Lawyers Very Nervous.
item.description: A trendy productivity hack, A.I. note takers are capturing every joke and offhand comment in many meetings. They could also potentially waive attorney-client privilege.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kessler

item.title: Frontier Jet Hits Person on Runway During Takeoff at Denver Airport
item.description: The plane reported an engine fire and aborted its takeoff. The people on board were safely evacuated.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Chris Hippensteel
item.media:description: A Frontier Airlines plane at Denver International Airport this month.

item.title: Which Trump Tariffs Are in Place, in the Works or Ruled Illegal
item.description: The president has reworked his tariffs repeatedly — sometimes because they have been declared illegal — with more updates still to come.
item.dc:creator: Lazaro Gamio and Tony Romm

item.title: How ‘The View’ Landed at the Center of a Free Speech Battle
item.description: The Trump administration’s focus on the show is testament to the enduring influence of an old-fashioned broadcast TV program started 29 years ago.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin and Jim Rutenberg
item.media:description: The hosts of ABC’s “The View,” from left, Whoopi Goldberg, Sara Haines, Joy Behar, Ana Navarro, Sunny Hostin and Alyssa Farah Griffin.

item.title: For Struggling JetBlue, Spirit’s Demise May Offer an Opportunity
item.description: JetBlue Airways is adding flights at Spirit Airlines’ former home airport as it tries to turn a profit again.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: With the closure of Spirit Airlines, JetBlue Airways is now the largest airline at Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport, near Miami.

item.title: Taiwan’s Plastic Habit Collides With Shortages Caused by a Faraway War
item.description: Taiwan, one of the world’s biggest plastic users, is facing supply disruptions caused by the war in Iran. The crunch is already trickling down into everyday life.
item.dc:creator: Xinyun Wu, Meaghan Tobin and An Rong Xu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: People use more plastic in Taiwan than almost anywhere else in the world. The average person uses at least one new plastic shopping bag a day, adding up to 10 billion plastic bags a year.

item.title: Retirees Expect Their Home to Be a Financial Safety Net. They Shouldn’t.
item.description: Older homeowners often can’t afford to maintain their houses or don’t understand the value in updating them. And that can mean thousands lost when they sell.
item.dc:creator: Martha C. White

item.title: When Mark Consuelos Wants to Escape New York, He Goes Here
item.description: “The midcentury architecture is fascinating. It’s also a place where I’m outside almost every waking hour of the day.”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.category: Live With Kelly and Mark (TV Program)

item.title: China’s Exports and Imports Set Records in April Amid High Energy Costs
item.description: The country’s exports surged and its trade surplus with the United States widened ahead of President Trump’s scheduled visit next week to Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the Beijing auto show in April, which featured hundreds of electric cars. Chinese electric vehicles are being exported in record numbers.

item.title: Norovirus Outbreak Sickens 115 on Cruise Ship
item.description: More than 100 passengers and 13 crew members on the Caribbean Princess reported being ill, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: The Caribbean Princess left Port Everglades in Fort Lauderdale, Fla., on April 28 and is expected to return to Florida on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Targets Iran’s Missile and Drone Program With Sanctions
item.description: The new measures are focused on Chinese companies that supply Iran’s military with materials to make drones.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Friday.

item.title: Lawsuit Accuses Lucky Strike of Building a Bowling Monopoly
item.description: The suit, filed by bowlers from several states, accuses Lucky Strike Entertainment of driving up prices and diminishing customers’ experiences across the country.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Evan Sung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lucky Strike’s alley in Times Square is one of the company’s more than 360 locations in North America.

item.title: Justice Dept. Settles Case Against Provider of Meat Industry Data
item.description: Under the agreement, Agri Stats would pay a fine and broaden whom it sold its pricing data to. The department said the move would help reduce food costs.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.media:description: The Justice Department said meat processors had used information exchanges run by Agri Stats to limit supply and raise prices.

item.title: Trump Plans to Fire F.D.A. Commissioner Marty Makary
item.description: Dr. Makary has been a supporter of the Make American Healthy Again Movement but made some enemies in the administration over vaping, the abortion pill and rejections of new drugs.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Maggie Haberman
item.media:description: Dr. Marty Makary, commissioner of the Food and Drug Administration, at the White House last year.

item.title: What to know about the report.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember

item.title: How Prepared Are We for A.I. Layoffs?
item.description: Our chief economics correspondent, Ben Casselman, describes how A.I.-related layoffs are testing the resilience of the government’s safety net programs.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman, Nour Idriss, Nikolay Nikolov, Stephanie Swart and Rafaela Balster

item.title: Trump’s Latest Tariff Setback Looms Over China Talks
item.description: For the president, a major legal defeat could undercut his leverage as he prepares to head to Beijing next week for high-stakes trade negotiations.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Ana Swanson
item.media:description: President Trump and Xi Jinping, the Chinese leader, in 2025. Tariffs are expected to be a major topic when they meet next week in Beijing.

item.title: Iran Says It Seized Oil Tanker Listed by U.S. as Sanctions Violator
item.description: A maritime tracking company said the move was likely performative, given the Chinese-owned ship has an “established history within the Iranian trade ecosystem.”
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ships in the Strait of Hormuz near Oman on Friday.

item.title: ABC Accuses Government of Violating First Amendment
item.description: The network’s argument, made to the F.C.C., is the most aggressive posture taken yet by a television network toward the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Jim Rutenberg and John Koblin
item.media:credit: Lanna Apisukh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The filing on Friday was registered on behalf of a single ABC station in Houston and involved a minor regulatory dispute over the talk show “The View,” which regularly hosts politicians and cultural figures.

item.title: Meta’s Embrace of A.I. Is Making Its Employees Miserable
item.description: As it adapts to the artificial intelligence era, the company is pushing many of its 78,000 workers to use the technology, and preparing to lay some of them off.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang, Eli Tan and Kate Conger

item.title: You May Be Owed a Tax Refund From the Covid Era
item.description: A court ruling found that the I.R.S. shouldn’t have charged penalties and interest during the pandemic. But the government may appeal the decision.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: What’s Happened So Far at the Musk v. OpenAI Trial
item.description: Elon Musk’s court battle against OpenAI has laid bare tensions between the start-up's leaders and raised questions about the billionaire’s motivations.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Mike Isaac
item.media:description: Outside the federal courthouse in Oakland, Calif., last week during the Elon Musk v. OpenAI trial.

item.title: Federal Reserve Turns Focus to Inflation as Job Market Stabilizes
item.description: April’s report showed employers added more jobs than expected, supporting the central bank’s view that it can afford to hold interest rates steady.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Higher prices could eventually temper consumer spending, causing a slowdown that could result in job losses.

item.title: With fuel costs soaring, Trump insists the economy is strong.
item.description: White House aides have sounded bullish on the jobs report, even as warning signs mount amid the war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Hassett, the director of the White House National Economic Council, was optimistic about the jobs report due Friday.

item.title: What to know about the report.
item.description: Employers added 115,000 jobs and the unemployment rate remained at 4.3 percent despite higher energy prices and instability spurred by the war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
